other_observers: Brenda Wright first heard the bird singing and in a few minutes we located the male.
description: Two adults birds with a singing male and a female in the area feeding mainly in Mock Orange. Brenda first heard the male singing as we had stopped at the pullout to read the signs. After seaching the shrubs we located the male below us. The female mostly stayed in the Mock Orange but did give us a few quick views.The male Black-chinned Sparrow mostly gray with a black face and chin, small pink bill, and dark eye. The back and tail were streaked and brown.The female looked much like the male except lacked the black about the face and head. If the Black-chinned Sparrow was not singing it could be mistaken for a Dark-eyed Junco. We had been aware of reports over the last three years of Black-chinned Sparrows on Mesa Verde National Park and were searching for this species on this occasion. We stopped at pullouts and would search for the sparrows in the shrubs. Mike Thompson had reported a singing Black-chinned Sparrow on a BBS survey a few weeks earlier south of our location in a different canyon complex on the opposite side of the road. This may suggest there could be other locations where Black-chinned Sparrows could be found in Mesa Verde National Park as well as other locations in southwest Colorado.
